items.statement: The pwd command prints the name of the current directory.
items.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
items.source_quote_or_summary: pwd prints the name of the current directory.

items.statement: The basename command removes any leading directory components from a name.
items.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
items.source_quote_or_summary: basename removes leading directory components from name.

items.statement: The dirname command prints all but the final slash-delimited component of each name.
items.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component
items.source_quote_or_summary: dirname prints all but the final slash-delimited component.

items.statement: If the input string contains no slash, dirname prints '.'.
conditions: The input string contains no slash.
items.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component
items.source_quote_or_summary: If the string contains no slash, dirname prints '.' meaning the current directory.

items.statement: The true command returns an exit status of 0, meaning success.
items.source_section: 16.2 true: Do nothing, successfully
items.source_quote_or_summary: true does nothing except return exit status 0, meaning success.
items.notes: The manual notes that in most modern shells, true is often a shell built-in.

items.statement: The false command returns an exit status of 1, meaning failure.
items.source_section: 16.1 false: Do nothing, unsuccessfully
items.source_quote_or_summary: false does nothing except return exit status 1, meaning failure.
items.notes: The manual notes that in most modern shells, false is often a shell built-in.

items.statement: With --logical, pwd outputs the contents of PWD if PWD is an absolute name of the current directory with no '.' or '..' components.
items.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
items.source_quote_or_summary: pwd --logical uses PWD when it is an absolute current-directory path without dot components.
items.notes: Behavior depends on the contents of the PWD environment variable.

items.statement: With --physical, pwd prints a fully resolved name for the current directory with no symbolic links in the printed path.
items.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
items.source_quote_or_summary: pwd --physical prints a fully resolved name with no symbolic links in the output path.

items.statement: If basename is given a suffix identical to the end of the name, that suffix is removed.
items.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
items.source_quote_or_summary: A suffix identical to the end of the name is removed by basename.

items.statement: The basename --zero option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
items.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
items.source_quote_or_summary: basename --zero uses an ASCII NUL terminator instead of a newline.

items.statement: The echo command writes each given string to standard output with a space between strings and a newline after the last one.
items.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
items.source_quote_or_summary: echo writes each string to standard output with spaces between strings and a trailing newline.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The echo -n option does not output the trailing newline.
items.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
items.source_quote_or_summary: echo -n suppresses the trailing newline.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The echo -e option enables interpretation of backslash-escaped characters in each string.
items.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
items.source_quote_or_summary: echo -e enables backslash escape interpretation.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The echo -E option disables interpretation of backslash escapes in each string.
items.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
items.source_quote_or_summary: echo -E disables backslash escape interpretation and is the default.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The printf command does formatted printing of text.
items.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data
items.source_quote_or_summary: printf does formatted printing of text.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The printf command prints the format string, interpreting percent directives and backslash escapes to format numeric and string arguments.
items.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data
items.source_quote_or_summary: printf interprets percent directives and backslash escapes in the format string.
items.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

items.statement: The yes command prints its command line arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, forever until it is killed.
items.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted
items.source_quote_or_summary: yes repeats its arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, until killed.

items.statement: If yes is invoked with no arguments, it prints 'y' followed by a newline forever until killed.
items.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted
items.source_quote_or_summary: With no arguments, yes prints y followed by a newline until killed.

items.statement: The printenv command prints environment variable values.
items.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
items.source_quote_or_summary: printenv prints environment variable values.

items.statement: If no variables are specified, printenv prints the value of every environment variable.
items.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
items.source_quote_or_summary: When no variable is specified, printenv prints every environment variable value.

items.statement: The printenv --null option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
items.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
items.source_quote_or_summary: printenv --null uses an ASCII NUL terminator instead of a newline.

items.statement: The tty command prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard input.
items.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input
items.source_quote_or_summary: tty prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard input.

items.statement: If standard input is not a terminal, tty prints 'not a tty'.
items.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input
items.source_quote_or_summary: tty prints not a tty when standard input is not a terminal.

items.statement: The logname command prints the calling user's name as found in a system-maintained file.
items.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name
items.source_quote_or_summary: logname prints the calling user's name from a system-maintained file.
items.notes: The manual cites files such as /var/run/utmp or /etc/utmp.

items.statement: If there is no entry for the calling process, logname exits with status 1.
items.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name
items.source_quote_or_summary: If there is no entry for the calling process, logname exits with status 1.

items.statement: The whoami command prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.
items.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name
items.source_quote_or_summary: whoami prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.

items.statement: The whoami command is equivalent to the command id -un.
items.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name
items.source_quote_or_summary: whoami is equivalent to id -un.

items.statement: The date command displays the date and time.
items.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time
items.source_quote_or_summary: date displays the date and time.
items.notes: Setting the system time depends on runtime privileges and environment.

items.statement: With the --set option, date sets the date and time before displaying it.
items.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time
items.source_quote_or_summary: With --set, date sets the date and time before displaying it.
items.notes: Setting the system time depends on runtime privileges and environment.

items.statement: If no options are given, uname acts as if the -s option were given.
items.source_section: 21.4 uname: Print system information
items.source_quote_or_summary: Without options, uname behaves as if -s were given.
